title: The Art of the Pentest Report 📄
description: Understand why reporting is the most important deliverable.

slides.title: The Hack is Useless Without the Report 📝
slides.content:
# The Most Important Deliverable

You popped a shell. You owned the Domain Admin. Great! But if you can't **communicate** that to the client in a way they understand, the engagement was a failure.

**Analogy**: A surgeon performing a brilliant operation but forgetting to stitch up the patient. The report **is** the stitch.

**What the Report Does:**
- **Communicates Risk**: Translates technical jargon (`CVE-2021-44228`) into business impact (`Attacker could steal all customer data`).
- **Provides Remediation**: Tells the IT team **exactly** how to fix the holes.
- **Legal Record**: Proves you stayed within scope and documented all actions.
- **Delivers Value**: The client paid for a **report**, not just a shell.

**Reality Check**: As a junior pentester, you will spend 30-50% of your time **writing reports**.

quizData.question: Why is the final report considered the most critical part of a penetration test?

options.text: It contains the most exciting screenshots of root shells.
It translates technical findings into actionable business risk and remediation steps.
It is required to get paid.
It is the longest part of the engagement.

quizData.explanation: The value of the test is the client's understanding of their risk and how to fix it.

slides.title: Anatomy of a Professional Pentest Report 🏗️
slides.content:
# Standard Industry Structure

Most firms follow a similar template:

1.  **Cover Page & Confidentiality Statement**
    - Client name, date, assessment type. "Confidential - For Internal Use Only."

2.  **Executive Summary** (1-2 pages)
    - **TL;DR for Executives**. Overall risk rating (Critical/High/Medium/Low). Key findings in plain English. Top 3 recommendations.

3.  **Methodology & Scope**
    - What you did (OWASP Top 10, PTES). What was in/out of scope. Timeframe.

4.  **Detailed Technical Findings**
    - **The Bulk of the Report**. Each vulnerability gets its own section:
        - Finding Title & Severity (CVSS)
        - Description
        - Impact
        - Evidence (Screenshots, HTTP Requests)
        - Remediation Steps

5.  **Appendices**
    - Full Nmap output, tool logs, exploit code (if applicable).

slides.title: Pro Tip: The 'Findings' Order 🚀
slides.content:
## Prioritize by Risk, Not Chronology

Don't list findings in the order you found them. Order by **Severity** (Critical -> High -> Medium -> Low -> Informational). The Executive Summary should only highlight the **Critical and High** risks. If you bury a Critical SQL Injection on page 47, the client will miss it.

slides.title: Confidentiality is Paramount ⚠️
slides.content:
## Secure the Report

A penetration test report contains a **blueprint for attacking the company**. Always encrypt the report (PDF password or PGP) and deliver it via a **secure channel** (e.g., company portal, encrypted email). Never send it to personal Gmail.
